topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The knock sounded brittle, a sharp interruption to the quiet drone of the afternoon. I shuffled to the door, my joints protesting with a dull ache that had settled deep in my bones all week. It was a package, taped clumsily, addressed to the former occupants. A sigh escaped me before I even reached for the letter opener. Another thing to manage, another loose end in this already frayed existence. I took the box, the cardboard surprisingly heavy, and carried it into the dim hallway. The air in the apartment felt thick, stagnant, mirroring the sluggishness in my own limbs. I set it on the worn welcome mat, the sound of the tape ripping echoing unnaturally loud in the silence. I didn't bother opening it right away; the energy required to deal with anything new seemed too much to summon. I just stood there, leaning against the doorframe, letting the weight of the unread contents settle onto my shoulders.

[story 2]

My eyes drifted shut halfway through the day, a familiar heaviness pulling at my eyelids. When the delivery person knocked, I blinked slowly, the effort feeling monumental. I moved toward the door with the deliberate slowness of someone wading through deep water. The package was there, an unwelcome intrusion. It was addressed to the people who had lived here before me. A faint, almost imperceptible tightening around my jaw was the only reaction I allowed myself. I took the parcel, my fingers brushing against the rough paper. Inside, there was just a few old photographs and a folded, brittle note. I carried it back to my small living space, the walk feeling like a marathon. I dropped it onto the chipped coffee table, sinking onto the sofa. The silence that followed was profound, a heavy blanket muffling any desire to move or speak.

[story 3]

The sound of the delivery chime seemed to scrape against the quiet of the evening. I didn't move immediately, just stared at the door, a dull throb behind my temples. It was just another chore, another piece of administrative nonsense dropped onto my doorstep. When I finally turned, the package sat there—a square of brown cardboard. My shoulders slumped further, a physical surrender to the day’s accumulation of effort. I picked it up; it felt impersonal, alien. The paper crinkled under my grip. I shuffled across the room, the floorboards groaning beneath my weight, and placed the item on the nearest surface. I didn't look inside. The simple act of receiving something meant another obligation, another task to process, and the sheer inertia of it made my muscles feel heavy, resistant to any further movement.
